question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 124km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (78kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (37kg each). The boat can travel at 48km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 13%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 14%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 54km waypoint, 7 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 62min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Now we need to simulate travel with possible rests every 9 km, and cannot operate more than 42 minutes continuously. So after 42 minutes of travel, must stop at next rest point? question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 111km. The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 15km is Zone A (speed limit: 38km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 36km waypoint, 5 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 40min, 3rd: 48min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 110km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 2 units of construction material (56kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 11%.
From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 63km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 25 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 25min, 2nd: 32min, 3rd: 39min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 115km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of medical kit (15kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 47km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 10%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 10%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 45km waypoint, 5 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 45min, 3rd: 51min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 110km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (58kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (20kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 34km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2100kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 13%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 10%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 36km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 27 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 27min, 2nd: 34min, 3rd: 41min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

So actual speed cannot exceed 34 km/h effective. So boat must adjust throttle such that effective speed = min(limit, possible). Since boat can go 44 km/h effective, but limited to 34 km/h.

Zone B rest 92 km (110-18). In Zone B current upstream, so effective speed = boat speed - current = 40 km/h. Speed limit in Zone B is 24 km/h effective.

Cargo weight: 13 units *58 = 754 kg. 5 units water barrel *20 = 100 kg. Total initial cargo = 854 kg. That's far below 2100 kg, so safety threshold not exceeded. So no reduction.

But later after unloading at 36 km waypoint (which is after 18km zone A + 18km into zone B? Actually waypoint at 36 km from start, so after first 18km zone A, then 18 km into zone B). At that point unload 3 water barrels (each 20kg) = 60 kg removed. Remaining cargo weight = 854 - 60 = 794 kg still below threshold.

Thus cargo regulation never triggers.

Congestion period 10:00-12:00: speed limits reduced by additional 10% (applied after cargo regulation). So during that time, effective speed limit in zones is multiplied by 0.9.

We need to consider travel schedule: start at 7:00 AM. Need to compute travel segments, factoring speed limits, possible rest stops, and rest duration increasing each rest.

Rest points every 9 km. So at distances: 9,18,27,36,45,54,63,72,81,90,99,108? Up to 110 final maybe not rest at 108? Rest points are designated; can only rest at those points. Must rest when continuous operation exceeds 42 minutes. So we need to schedule rests accordingly.

First segment: 0 to 9 km (within Zone A). Effective speed limit = 34 km/h (no cargo reduction). Not in congestion period until 10:00. So speed = 34 km/h. Time = distance / speed = 9 km / 34 km/h = 0.2647059 h = 15.88235 minutes ≈ 15.88 min. That's less than 42, okay.

Second segment: 9 to 18 km (still Zone A). Same speed 34 km/h. Distance 9 km, time 15.88 min. Cumulative since last rest: 31.76 min (<42). At 18 km is a rest point, also end of Zone A. At 18 km we may need to consider rest? Not mandatory unless 42 min reached. Not yet, but we could continue.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 13% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 15%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 36km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 38 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 38min, 2nd: 46min, 3rd: 54min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 11%.
From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 90km waypoint, 4 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 48min, 3rd: 56min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 117km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 15 units of water barrel (22kg each) and 2 units of relief supply box (46kg each). The boat can travel at 47km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 34km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.
From 12:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 45km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 46min, 3rd: 53min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 108km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (60kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (69kg each). The boat can travel at 51km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 15km is Zone A (speed limit: 37km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 11%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 14%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 90km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 55min, 3rd: 63min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 104km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of relief supply box (66kg each) and 3 units of medical kit (7kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.
From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 27km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 26 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 26min, 2nd: 33min, 3rd: 40min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 113km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (45kg each) and 5 units of construction material (53kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 17km is Zone A (speed limit: 33km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 90km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 30 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 30min, 2nd: 38min, 3rd: 46min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

We need to compute travel time over 113 km with zones: first 17 km Zone A, then remainder 96 km Zone B (since total 113 km). Actually 113-17 = 96 km.

Speed parameters: boat speed in still water = 42 km/h. Current 2 km/h. Effective speed = boat speed +/- current. So in upstream (Zone A) effective raw speed = 42 - 2 = 40 km/h. In downstream (Zone B) effective raw speed = 42 + 2 = 44 km/h.

But there are speed limits (max effective speed) per zone: Zone A limit 33 km/h, Zone B limit 23 km/h. These limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current. So we must ensure effective speed does not exceed limit.

We also have cargo weight threshold: safety weight threshold 2400 kg. If cargo exceeds, speed limits reduced: Zone A limit reduced by 11%, Zone B reduced by 15%.

We need to compute cargo weight initially: 10 units relief supply box 45 kg each = 450 kg. 5 units construction material 53 kg each = 265 kg. Total initial cargo weight = 715 kg. That's far below 2400 kg, so no reduction initially.

But note: At 90 km waypoint, unload 2 units of construction material (2*53 = 106 kg). After unloading, cargo weight reduces; still below threshold, so no reduction anyway.

Thus cargo regulation does not affect speed limits at any time (since weight never exceeds 2400). So speed limits remain 33 km/h (Zone A) and 23 km/h (Zone B), unless congestion period reduces further.

Congestion period: from 10:00 to 12:00, speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13% (applied on top of any cargo reduction). So during that interval, effective speed limit = original limit * (1 - 0.13) = 0.87 * limit.

Thus need to compute travel time with variable speed limits depending on time of day, also limited by max effective speed (cannot exceed limit). Also we must consider that boat cannot operate continuously >45 minutes; must rest at designated rest points every 9 km. Rest points are at distances: 0? Starting point maybe not rest. Rest points at 9 km, 18 km, 27 km, 36 km, 45 km, 54 km, 63 km, 72 km, 81 km, 90 km, 99 km, 108 km, etc. Up to 113 km final.

But note rest can only be taken at designated rest points, every 9 km. Also cannot operate >45 minutes continuously, so must rest before exceeding that.

We need to schedule rests accordingly, factoring travel speeds (which may change after unloading at 90 km). Also rest duration increases cumulatively: first rest 30 min, second 38 min, third 46 min, etc (increase by 8 min each time). We need to count number of rests taken.

We need to compute total travel time including rests, starting at 7:00 AM departure.

We need to compute travel segments between rest points (or destination) with given speeds, and ensure each segment travel time <=45 minutes; if a segment would exceed 45 min, need to insert rest earlier? But rest points are every 9 km, so we can stop at each rest point; we can also possibly skip some if travel time between two rest points is less than 45 min. However rule: "The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km)." That suggests you can travel up to 45 min, but you may only rest at those points. So you could travel multiple intervals (9km each) without rest as long as total time since last rest <=45 min. But you cannot exceed 45 min before a rest; if you would exceed, you must rest at the previous rest point.

Thus we need to simulate travel, accumulating time, checking if next segment would cause cumulative continuous operation >45 min; if so, you must rest at current point before proceeding.

We also must consider speed changes due to congestion period (10-12). So speed limit may be lower during that interval, causing longer travel times.

Also at 90 km unload 2 units; but weight still below threshold, no effect.

The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 13%.
From 1:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 108km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 42min, 3rd: 50min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.
From 1:00 PM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 81km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 46 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 50min, 3rd: 58min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 43 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 43min, 2nd: 51min, 3rd: 59min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.
From 1:00 PM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 15%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 63km waypoint, 8 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 38 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 38min, 2nd: 44min, 3rd: 50min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 108km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (64kg each). The boat can travel at 40km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 17km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 10%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 14%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 27km waypoint, 5 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 30 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 30min, 2nd: 37min, 3rd: 44min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
thinking_content:
We need to compute travel time for 108 km upstream (overall upstream region). Boat speed in still water 40 km/h. River current 2 km/h. Zone A first 17 km upstream, current upstream meaning effective speed = boat speed - current = 40 - 2 = 38 km/h (but speed limits apply to effective speed). Speed limit in Zone A is 30 km/h (effective). However cargo weight may reduce limit. Also congestion period 9-11 reduces limits further.

Zone B after 17 km to end (108-17 = 91 km). In Zone B current downstream, so effective speed = boat speed + current = 40 + 2 = 42 km/h. Speed limit in Zone B is 24 km/h effective.

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 112km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 8 units of medical kit (13kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (37kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 39min, 3rd: 46min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 36km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 12%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 63km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 58min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 112km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (54kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (24kg each). The boat can travel at 47km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 13%.
From 11:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 1 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 31 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 31min, 2nd: 39min, 3rd: 47min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 8 units of relief supply box (69kg each) and 2 units of construction material (56kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 10%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 15%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 81km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 43 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 43min, 2nd: 49min, 3rd: 55min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 104km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 5 units of construction material (56kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (20kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.
From 11:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 90km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 38 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 38min, 2nd: 45min, 3rd: 52min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Zone B next 81 km (since total 104 km) downstream, effective speed = boat speed + current = 42 + 2 = 44 km/h.

But speed limits: Zone A limit 25 km/h (effective speed). Zone B limit 25 km/h (effective speed). So the boat must not exceed those effective speeds.

Cargo weight: 5 units *56kg = 280 kg; 6 units *20kg = 120 kg; total = 400 kg. That's far below safety threshold 1700 kg, so cargo weight does NOT exceed threshold initially, so no reduction.

But later after unloading at 90km, cargo weight reduces further, still below threshold, so no reduction either. So cargo regulation does not apply at any point. So speed limits remain 25 km/h (effective) unless congestion period.

Congestion period: 11:00-12:00, speed limits in all zones reduced by additional 11% (applied on top of speed limit after cargo regulation). So during that hour, effective speed limit = 25 km/h * (1 - 0.11) = 22.25 km/h.

We need to consider that the boat cannot operate continuously >43 minutes. Rest points every 9 km (designated). Rest durations: first rest 38 min, then each subsequent rest adds 7 min (45,52,...). Rest only at rest points (every 9 km). Need to schedule rests along route given max continuous operation.

We need to compute travel time including rests, respecting speed limits (effective). Also need to consider congestion period: if the boat is traveling during 11-12, speed limit reduced. If it is resting during that period, speed limit doesn't matter.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 35 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 35min, 2nd: 41min, 3rd: 47min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 130km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 8 units of construction material (57kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (24kg each). The boat can travel at 45km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 13% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 11%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 40km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 39min, 3rd: 46min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
thinking_content:
We need to compute total travel time including rests. Steps: Determine cargo weight initially, check if exceeds 1900kg, apply speed limit reductions. Then travel along 130 km with zones: first 25 km Zone A, then remaining 105 km Zone B. However there is an unloading at 40 km waypoint (which is in Zone B, since Zone A ends at 25 km). So after 40 km, cargo weight reduces, potentially affecting speed limits for remaining distance.

Also congestion period 11:00-13:00 reduces speed limits by 11% extra, applied on top of cargo reduction.

Boat cannot operate continuously more than 42 minutes. Rest points every 8 km (i.e., at distances 8,16,24,32,40,48,...). Rest only permitted at those points. Rest duration base 32 min, each subsequent rest adds 7 min.

We need to simulate timeline: start 8:00 AM, travel, possibly encounter congestion period, speed limit changes, rest constraints.

We must compute travel time in minutes (including rests). Let's proceed stepwise.

First compute cargo weight:

Construction material: 8 units *57kg = 456 kg
Water barrels: 6 units *24kg = 144 kg
Total initial weight = 600 kg. That's far below 1900 kg, so cargo does NOT exceed safety threshold. So no speed limit reduction due to cargo initially.

But we must ensure that effective speed cannot exceed boat's capability: Boat speed in still water =45 km/h. In Zone A effective speed = boat speed + current = 45+3 =48 km/h. However speed limit is 35 km/h, so boat must travel at effective speed 35 km/h (i.e., adjust throttle). In Zone B effective speed = boat speed - current =45-3=42 km/h, limit 23 km/h, so travel at 23 km/h effective.

Now after unloading at 40 km, cargo weight reduces: unload 5 units of construction material = 5*57=285 kg. Remaining cargo: originally 8 units => 3 units left (3*57=171 kg) plus water barrels 6*24=144 kg => total 315 kg. Still below threshold, so no speed reduction.

Thus speed limits unchanged throughout.

Now we need to incorporate congestion period 11:00-13:00 where speed limits reduced by additional 11% (applied on top of cargo reduction, but none). So during that time, effective speed limits become 35 * (1 -0.11)= 31.15 km/h in Zone A, and 23 *0.89 = 20.47 km/h in Zone B.

Now we need to compute travel timeline with rests.

Rest points every 8 km. Starting at 0 km, we can rest at 8,16,24,32,40,48,56,64,72,80,88,96,104,112,120,128 (but destination at 130 km, so maybe not at 128 because need 2 km after). The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 27km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 36 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 36min, 2nd: 44min, 3rd: 52min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 114km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 7 units of construction material (74kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (42kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.
From 12:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 14%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 36km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 49min, 3rd: 57min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

But speed limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current. So max allowed effective speed is limit, not boat's capability. So we must use the lower of boat's effective speed and speed limit (after reductions). Also need to consider cargo weight threshold 1900 kg: if exceeds, speed limits reduced (percentage). Then congestion period (12-2) further reduces limits by 14% (applied after cargo reduction). Need to compute speed limits for each segment depending on time of travel (if within congestion period). Also rest stops: cannot operate continuously >43 minutes. Must rest at designated rest points every 9 km. Base rest duration 41 min, each subsequent rest adds 8 min.

We need total travel time from start at 9:00 AM to reach 114 km downstream? Actually upstream region spanning total of 114 km. Starting point presumably at downstream end, traveling upstream? Now speed limits: Zone A limit 28 km/h, Zone B limit 23 km/h. These limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current. So the max allowed effective speed is the limit (or reduced). But after unloading 1 unit at 36km waypoint (relief box weight 42 kg), cargo weight becomes 686 kg, still below threshold. So cargo regulation never triggers. So speed limits remain original (except congestion reduction).

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 116km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of medical kit (15kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (63kg each). The boat can travel at 44km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 16%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 27km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 43 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 43min, 2nd: 50min, 3rd: 57min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 109km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 9 units of relief supply box (53kg each) and 3 units of construction material (73kg each). The boat can travel at 41km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 13%.
From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 62min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
thinking_content:
We need to compute travel time with varying speed limits, cargo weight affecting speed limits, congestion period reduction, rest stops every 9km, max continuous operation 44 minutes, rest durations increasing.

We need to track position, speed, time, rests.

Given: total distance 109 km upstream. Boat speed in still water = 41 km/h. River current 2 km/h. Zone A upstream (first 16 km) effective speed = boat speed - current = 41 - 2 = 39 km/h (before limits). Zone B downstream (rest of distance) effective speed = boat speed + current = 41 + 2 = 43 km/h (before limits).

But speed limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current. So we have base speed limits: Zone A limit 26 km/h, Zone B limit 23 km/h. If cargo exceeds safety threshold 2400 kg, reduce limits: Zone A by 12%, Zone B by 13%. Then congestion period (12:00-13:00) further reduces limits by 17% (applied after cargo reduction).

We need to compute cargo weight: initial cargo: 9 units relief supply box each 53 kg = 9*53 = 477 kg. 3 units construction material each 73 kg = 219 kg. Total initial weight = 477+219 = 696 kg. That's far below 2400 kg, so cargo threshold not exceeded. So no reduction initially.

At 72 km waypoint, unload 2 units of construction material (2*73=146 kg). After unloading, cargo weight reduces to 696-146 = 550 kg, still below threshold. So no cargo reduction ever.

Thus speed limits remain as base, except during congestion period we reduce by 17%.

Now need to consider rest points every 9 km. Starting point at 0 km. Rest points at 9,18,27,36,45,54,63,72,81,90,99,108? Destination at 109 km, so last rest point at 108 km (since every 9 km). Rest can only be taken at those points. Also cannot operate continuously more than 44 minutes; if travel time between rests exceeds 44 min, must stop earlier? The rule: "The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km)." So you must ensure each segment between rests (or start to first rest, etc.) does not exceed 44 minutes. If a segment would take longer than 44 minutes, you would need to stop earlier? But rest only at designated points, so you must plan such that travel between consecutive rest points is <=44 min; if not, you might need to insert extra rest? But not allowed except at those points. So we must check each segment length and speed to see travel time; if >44 min, you must perhaps stop at the earlier rest point (which is the same point). Actually you travel from one rest point to next (9 km). Compute time = distance / speed (effective speed limited by speed limit). If that time >44 min, you cannot travel that segment without violating continuous operation. Then you would need to stop earlier? But not allowed. So perhaps you would need to take a rest at the start point before proceeding? But you start at 0 with no rest needed. The rule likely means you cannot travel more than 44 minutes without a rest, so you must schedule rests accordingly. Since rests only at 9 km intervals, you must ensure each segment's travel time <=44 min. If any segment exceeds, you must perhaps split segment by taking a rest at the start point? Not possible. So maybe we need to compute travel times and see if any exceed 44 min; if they do, we must adjust by adding extra rest (maybe at same point? Not allowed). Might be that all segments will be under 44 min given speeds.

Let's compute speeds.

Effective speed limited by speed limit: The boat's effective speed (after current) cannot exceed speed limit. So actual speed = min(effective speed without limit, speed limit). Since effective speeds: Zone A 39 km/h, Zone B 43 km/h. Limits: 26 km/h (Zone A) and 23 km/h (Zone B). So limits are lower, so actual speed = 26 km/h in Zone A, 23 km/h in Zone B (unless congestion reduces further). During congestion (12-13), reduce those limits by 17%: Zone A speed limit becomes 26 * (1-0.17)=26*0.83=21.58 km/h. Zone B becomes 23*0.83=19.09 km/h.

Now we need to compute timeline with possible crossing of congestion period.

So we can plan rests to minimize total rest time while respecting 44 min limit.

Thus we should schedule rests such that each continuous travel segment (between rests) is ≤44 min, and rests are at multiples of 9 km.

We also need to consider that after each rest, the rest duration increments by 8 minutes (cumulative). So if we take many rests, total rest time grows.

Goal: total travel time including mandatory rests. Likely we want to minimize number of rests, i.e., take as few rests as possible while respecting 44 min limit.

Let's compute travel times per 9 km segment in each zone and during congestion.

Now we need to consider congestion period 12:00-13:00. Let's compute timeline.

Now we decide about rests. Since we can travel up to 44 min continuously, we could continue without rest to next rest point at 18 km, which would take additional 21.371 min, total continuous time = 20.769 + 21.371 = 42.14 min, still under 44. Now next rest point at 27 km. Segment 18-27 km lies fully in Zone B (since after 16 km). So speed 23 km/h, time = 9/23 = 23.478 min.

If we continue without rest, continuous travel from start would be 42.14 + 23.478 = 65.618 min >44, not allowed. So we must have rested at some point before exceeding 44 min. Since we didn't rest at 9 km, we must rest at 18 km to reset continuous timer. So we will rest at 18 km.

Now after rest, continuous timer resets. Next we can travel further.

Now after 18 km, time is 9:42.14 + 46 min rest = 10:28.14 (10:28:08). Continue.

Segment 18-27 km: 23.478 min, arrival at 27 km at 10:51.618 (10:51:37).

Now we could continue to 36 km (next rest point). Segment 27-36 km also 9 km in Zone B: 23.478 min. Total continuous from after rest: 23.478 + 23.478 = 46.956 min >44, so we cannot travel two segments without rest. So we must rest at 27 km.

Thus rest at 27 km: this will be second rest, duration 54 min.

Arrival at 27 km at 10:51.618, rest 54 min -> depart at 11:45.618 (11:45:37).

Now continue.

Segment 27-36 km: 23.478 min, arrival at 36 km at 12:09.096 (12:09:06). Note that this passes into congestion period (12:00-13:00). The segment started at 11:45.618, traveled 23.478 min, so part before 12:00 and part after.

We need to compute actual travel time considering speed reduction during 12-13. Since speed limit reduces at 12:00, the speed for the portion after 12:00 is lower (19.09 km/h). So we need to split the segment.

Let's compute distance covered before 12:00.

Start segment at 11:45.618. Time until 12:00 = 14.382 minutes (12:00 - 11:45.618). At what speed? Before 12:00, speed limit is 23 km/h. So distance covered in 14.382 min = speed * time.

Convert speed to km per minute: 23 km/h = 23/60 = 0.38333 km/min.

Distance = 0.38333 * 14.382 = 5.514 km approx.

The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 33km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 122km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 5 units of water barrel (25kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (5kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 33km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 16%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 1 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 46 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 61min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 13% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 1:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 29 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 29min, 2nd: 35min, 3rd: 41min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 102km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (79kg each) and 3 units of relief supply box (54kg each). The boat can travel at 48km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 38km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2100kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 12%.
From 12:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 12%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 36km waypoint, 4 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 31 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 31min, 2nd: 38min, 3rd: 45min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

In Zone A (downstream current) effective speed = boat speed + current = 48+2 = 50 km/h (if moving downstream). But they are traveling upstream overall? Actually they are moving upstream region, but zones have different current direction relative to boat? It says "In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed)." So in Zone A, current helps, effective speed = 48+2 = 50 km/h. In Zone B, current opposes, effective speed = 48-2 = 46 km/h. However speed limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current: Zone A limit 38 km/h, Zone B limit 24 km/h. So they cannot exceed those effective speeds.

If cargo exceeds safety weight threshold (2100kg), then Zone A speed limit reduced by 14% and Zone B by 12%. Need to compute cargo weight: initially 9 units *79 = 711 kg, plus 3 units *54 = 162 kg. Total = 873 kg. That's far below 2100 kg, so no reduction initially.

But after unloading at 36 km waypoint, they unload 4 units of construction material (each 79 kg) = 316 kg removed. So remaining cargo weight = 873 - 316 = 557 kg, still below threshold. So cargo regulation never triggers.

Now congestion period 12:00-14:00 reduces speed limits by additional 12% (applied on top of cargo reduction). Since cargo reduction not applied, we just reduce base limits by 12% during that period.

Now we have to consider rest constraints: cannot operate continuously >43 minutes. Rest allowed only at designated rest points every 9 km (i.e., at km 9,18,27,36,45,54,63,72,81,90,99?). question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 114km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 2 units of relief supply box (33kg each). The boat can travel at 44km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 36km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.
From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 99km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 42min, 3rd: 50min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 28 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 28min, 2nd: 36min, 3rd: 44min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 16%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 36km waypoint, 1 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 62min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 102km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 9 units of water barrel (24kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (15kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 125km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 11 units of medical kit (7kg each) and 3 units of construction material (57kg each). The boat can travel at 41km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 33km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 11%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 36km waypoint, 4 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 46 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 26 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 26min, 2nd: 33min, 3rd: 40min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 10%.
From 9:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 9 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 46 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 37 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 37min, 2nd: 43min, 3rd: 49min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 108km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 7 units of construction material (62kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 11%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 15%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 54km waypoint, 1 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 44 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 44min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 60min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
