The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2100kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 44 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 44min, 2nd: 51min, 3rd: 58min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 124km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 15 units of construction material (74kg each) and 3 units of relief supply box (30kg each). The boat can travel at 52km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 36km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 45km waypoint, 10 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 29 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 29min, 2nd: 35min, 3rd: 41min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

At the 54km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 33 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 33min, 2nd: 39min, 3rd: 45min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 124km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of relief supply box (42kg each) and 3 units of medical kit (14kg each). The boat can travel at 50km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 38km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 12%.
From 1:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 99km waypoint, 1 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 55min, 3rd: 63min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

We need to compute travel time with varying speed limits, cargo weight affecting speed limits, congestion period reduction, rest stops every 9 km, cannot operate more than 43 minutes continuously. Rest durations increase cumulatively.

We need to simulate the trip from 0 km to 124 km.

Boat speed in still water: 50 km/h. River current 3 km/h.

Effective speed = boat speed +/- current depending on zone.

But speed limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current. So the boat cannot exceed the speed limit (effective speed). So actual effective speed = min(boat's effective speed (boat speed +/- current), speed limit after reductions). Since boat speed is high (50), effective speed upstream = 47 km/h, downstream = 53 km/h. Speed limits are lower (38,24). So speed limit will be binding.

Thus effective speed = speed limit after cargo and congestion reductions.

We need to compute cargo weight: 11 units relief 42kg each = 462 kg. 3 units medical 14kg each = 42 kg. Total initial weight = 504 kg. That's far below safety threshold 1600 kg, so no reduction? Wait threshold 1600 kg. So cargo does NOT exceed threshold, so no reduction.

Thus initial speed limits: Zone A: 38 km/h. Zone B: 24 km/h.

Congestion period 1:00-2:00 PM reduces speed limits by additional 18% (multiply by (1-0.18)=0.82) after cargo reduction (none). So during that hour, limits become: Zone A: 38*0.82 = 31.16 km/h. Zone B: 24*0.82 = 19.68 km/h.

Now we have rest constraints: cannot operate more than 43 minutes continuously. Must rest at designated rest points every 9 km (i.e., at km markers 9,18,27,36,45,54,63,72,81,90,99,108,117,126?). Destination at 124 km, so last rest point before destination is at 117 km. Also there is a waypoint at 99 km where unload 1 medical kit (14 kg). After unloading, cargo weight reduces to 490 kg, still below threshold, so no speed limit changes.

Thus cargo weight never exceeds threshold, so speed limits unchanged except congestion.

We need to compute travel segments between rest points, ensuring each segment travel time does not exceed 43 minutes. If a segment distance would take >43 minutes at applicable speed, we may need to stop earlier? But rest only allowed at designated points every 9 km. So we must possibly add extra rest at the next rest point even if we exceed 43 minutes? Actually rule: "The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km)." This implies you must plan so that travel between rests does not exceed 43 minutes. If a segment between two rest points would take longer than 43 minutes, then you would have to stop earlier, but not allowed. So perhaps the distances are such that travel times are less than 43 minutes given speeds.

Let's compute travel times per 9 km segment in each zone.

Segment speed depends on zone: Zone A first 24 km. So rest points at 9km, 18km within Zone A. Then at 24km is zone boundary (not a rest point unless 24 is multiple of 9? 24 not multiple of 9). Next rest point is 27km (which is in Zone B). So we need to handle segment from 18 to 27 (9 km crossing zone boundary at 24). However speed limit may change mid-segment. We need to possibly split segment at zone boundary.

Thus we need to compute travel along the route, possibly breaking at zone boundary and at congestion period times.

Also need to consider time of day to know when congestion period occurs (13:00-14:00). Starting at 9:00.

We also need to account for rest durations increasing: first rest 47 min, second 55, third 63, etc.

We need to schedule rests at each rest point encountered, unless maybe the boat reaches destination before needing rest? The rule: rest is only permitted at designated rest points, but not required? "The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes." So if travel time to next rest point would exceed 43 min, must rest earlier, but not allowed. So we must rest at each rest point, because distance 9 km at speed limits likely yields travel time less than 43 min. But do we need to rest at every rest point, or can we skip? The rule says rest is only permitted at designated rest points, not that you must rest there. However you cannot exceed 43 min continuous operation, so you could potentially travel multiple rest points before needing rest if total time under 43 min. But you are allowed to rest only at those points; you could also choose not to rest. Thus we need to simulate time progression, track distance, speed limit depending on zone and congestion, and enforce rest after 43 min of travel (not counting rest time). If you exceed 43 minutes before reaching next rest point, that would be illegal; we must ensure schedule such that you rest at or before 43 minutes. So we may need to adjust speed? Not possible. Travel 9 km at 24 km/h takes 0.375 h = 22.5 minutes. That's less than 43. Even with reduction to 19.68 km/h, time = 9 / 19.68 = 0.457 h = 27.4 min. Still less than 43. So you could travel multiple rest points before needing rest. So we can accumulate up to 43 minutes before resting, possibly covering multiple 9 km segments.

Thus we need to schedule rests at times when 43 minutes of cumulative travel have elapsed, and that point must be at a rest point. Since travel times per segment are less than 43, we can continue until we exceed 43, then we may be somewhere within a segment. To avoid that, we could plan to stop at the next rest point after exceeding 43, but then we would have traveled >43 minutes continuously, which violates. So we must ensure we stop before exceeding 43 minutes. That means we need to rest at the last rest point before the 43 minute limit is reached, even if we could have gone further. So we need to track cumulative travel time since last rest; when adding next segment would exceed 43, we must stop before that segment (i.e., at current rest point). So we need to decide at each rest point whether to rest now or continue.

At each rest point (multiple of 9 km), compute travel time from previous point (or start) to this point using appropriate speed limits (which may change within segment). Add to cumulative travel since last rest. If cumulative would exceed 43 after adding this segment, then we must have rested at previous rest point (i.e., we should have rested earlier). So we need to decide earlier. Since we cannot retroactively, we need to plan ahead: before traveling a segment, check if cumulative + segment time > 43. If yes, then we must rest at current location before traveling that segment (i.e., at previous rest point). So we will rest at previous rest point, reset cumulative, then travel segment.

Thus we will rest at some rest points, not necessarily all.

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 117km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 11 units of medical kit (13kg each) and 5 units of construction material (50kg each). The boat can travel at 51km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 36km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 54km waypoint, 4 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 55min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 13% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 11:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 15%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 54km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 46 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 29 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 29min, 2nd: 37min, 3rd: 45min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 14%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 45km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 44 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 44min, 2nd: 50min, 3rd: 56min, ...).

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 33km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 12%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 90km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 48min, 3rd: 54min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 101km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 8 units of medical kit (15kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 45km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 15km is Zone A (speed limit: 37km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 12:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 14%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 81km waypoint, 1 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 54min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
thinking_content:
We need to compute travel time for 101 km upstream (overall upstream? Actually moving upstream overall? The river has zones: first 15 km Zone A (upstream current), then Zone B (downstream current). Since traveling upstream overall, but zone B current is downstream, which helps? Actually effective speed = boat speed +/- current. Boat speed in still water = 45 km/h. Current speed = 2 km/h. So in Zone A (upstream), effective speed = 45 - 2 = 43 km/h before speed limit. In Zone B (downstream), effective speed = 45 + 2 = 47 km/h before speed limit.

But speed limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current. So we must ensure effective speed does not exceed zone speed limit (which may be reduced due to cargo and congestion). So actual speed = min(effective speed, speed limit). However, we may also be able to adjust throttle to meet limit. So travel speed = limited speed.

We need to consider cargo weight: medical kits 8 units *15kg = 120 kg. Water barrels 3 units *18kg = 54 kg. Total initial cargo weight = 174 kg. That's far below 1700 kg threshold. So cargo does NOT exceed safety weight threshold. So no reduction initially. Wait threshold is 1700 kg, we are at 174 kg, so no reduction. So speed limits remain as given (37 km/h in Zone A, 23 km/h in Zone B) unless cargo later exceeds? Not exceed. So no cargo reduction.

But after unloading 1 water barrel at 81 km waypoint, cargo weight reduces further, still below threshold, no effect.

Thus cargo regulation irrelevant.

Now congestion period 12:00-14:00 reduces speed limits by 14% (applied on top of any cargo reduction). So during that time, speed limits are multiplied by (1 - 0.14) = 0.86.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 55min, ...).

Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 110km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 5 units of medical kit (5kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (51kg each). The boat can travel at 50km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 37km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 9:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 38min, 3rd: 44min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 15%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 63km waypoint, 1 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 35 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 35min, 2nd: 43min, 3rd: 51min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.

At the 81km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 33 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 33min, 2nd: 39min, 3rd: 45min, ...).

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 110km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of water barrel (18kg each) and 2 units of relief supply box (31kg each). The boat can travel at 45km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 4 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 45 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 45min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 61min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 45km waypoint, 10 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 29 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 29min, 2nd: 36min, 3rd: 43min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The first 17km is Zone A (speed limit: 34km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 12%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 36km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 50min, 3rd: 58min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
